title: Eco printing with leaves
subtitle: Steaming leaves onto alum-mordanted wool to transfer their tannins and pigments
excerpt: Eco printing (also called botanical printing) places fresh leaves directly onto mordanted wet wool, rolls the bundle tightly, and steams it. The tannins, pigments, and mineral compounds in the leaves transfer directly onto the fibre as contact prints, leaving leaf-shaped marks in greens, ochres, rusts, and greys.
sourceNotes: Eco printing on textiles is a technique associated with India Flint, documented in her book 'Eco Colour' (2008). The contact-print method on mordanted fabric is described consistently in natural dyeing texts. Observation-led.

content.text: , leaves are placed directly on mordanted wet fibre and the whole bundle is steamed. Heat and pressure drive the plant pigments and tannins into the fibre. The best results come from
collected in autumn when tannin content is highest. Autumn is the most productive season, though many leaves give prints in summer as well. The finished fabric is a direct map of the leaf contact.

items.qty: up to 50 x 50 cm per bundle
items.name: Alum-mordanted wool felt or woven fabric, pre-wetted
Fresh leaves (oak, sweet chestnut, rose, eucalyptus, Japanese maple)
Iron modifier solution (optional): a few iron nails soaked in water for 3 days
String or rubber bands for securing the roll
Bamboo dowel or thick stick for rolling (30-40 cm)
Stainless steel steaming pot with a rack or trivet

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the fabric and leaves

Lay the damp mordanted fabric flat on a clean surface. For stronger prints, brush the fabric lightly with iron modifier solution using a soft brush: iron reacts with tannins to produce darker, greyer marks on the print boundary. Lay leaves face-down on one half of the fabric, arranged with space between them. Fold the empty half of the fabric over the leaves to sandwich them.
Place the dowel at one edge of the folded fabric and roll the whole sandwich around it firmly, keeping tension even. The
must be firm: loose rolls give faint, uneven prints. Bind the roll with string at 5-10 cm intervals to keep it compressed.

Place the bundle on a rack above simmering water in the steaming pot. The bundle should not touch the water. Cover with a tight-fitting lid and steam for 60-90 minutes, checking water level every 20 minutes. Long steaming gives deeper prints. Do not unroll the bundle until it has cooled for at least 30 minutes.

Unroll carefully: the leaves may be fragile and stuck to the fabric. Peel them away gently. The fabric underneath should show leaf-shaped prints in various ochres, rusts, greens, and greys. Rinse the fabric in cold water to remove loose plant matter. Dry flat in shade. The prints will lighten slightly on drying.

items.fix: Use freshly picked leaves. Dampen the fabric thoroughly before layering. Roll the bundle as firmly as possible and bind tightly. Steam for the full 90 minutes.
items.cause: The leaves were too dry, the fabric was not damp enough, or the bundle was not rolled tightly enough.
items.symptom: Prints are very faint or barely visible

items.fix: Leave space between leaves in the next bundle, and ensure the fabric sandwich is folded cleanly between leaves.
items.cause: The leaves were packed too closely, allowing tannins to spread between adjacent leaves during steaming.
items.symptom: Prints bleed into each other with no clear leaf outline
